title: Ectopic Pregnancy — Ruptured
oneLiner: Haemorrhagic emergency from tubal rupture in a woman of reproductive age with positive pregnancy test — requires immediate surgical control of bleeding.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Haemorrhagic shock:** hypotension + tachycardia unresponsive to 2L crystalloid = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise. Single finding: postural hypotension (lying -> sitting BP drop >20 mmHg) with abdominal tenderness and positive pregnancy test.
- **Massive intra-abdominal bleeding:** loss of consciousness / peritonism / distended ballotable uterus sign (Cullen’s sign is late). Single finding: **free fluid in Morrison's pouch on FAST ultrasound** in a shocked pregnant woman.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Lower abdominal/pelvic pain (sudden, severe, unilateral → diffuse)
  - Amenorrhoea (6–8 weeks) with positive pregnancy test
  - Vaginal bleeding (often scant, brown, variable)
  - Syncope / presyncope / shoulder tip pain (due to phrenic irritation from blood)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal: tenderness ± rigidity, guarding, rebound; distension with ballotable uterus if large haemoperitoneum
  - Pelvic: cervical excitation (pulling of cervix causes sharp pain), adnexal tenderness ± mass
  - Vital signs: tachycardia ± hypotension (late sign), postural BP drop, narrow pulse pressure
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Anaesthetic ectopic / tubal abortion** → mild pain, no shock, small adnexal mass on USS
- **Miscarriage (threatened / incomplete)** → central pain, open cervical os, intrauterine products on USS
- **Ovarian cyst rupture** → sudden pain but negative pregnancy test (key differentiator)
- **Appendicitis** → migrating pain, anorexia, negative pregnancy test (or intrauterine pregnancy on USS)
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** → bilateral pain, cervical discharge, fever, positive STI screen
- **Ureteric colic** → flank-to-groin pain, haematuria, negative pregnancy test
  - **Splitter:** the most reliable single feature that bifurcates ruptured ectopic from all others is **positive pregnancy test + free fluid in pelvis** on bedside ultrasound. If positive test and no intrauterine pregnancy on transvaginal USS → ectopic until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine β-hCG (if serum not yet back) — **MUST do in every woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain**
  - Two large-bore IV cannulae
  - ECG (tachycardia, but not diagnostic)
  - Bedside FAST / limited pelvic ultrasound if trained (detect free fluid, adnexal mass)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia from occult bleed; Hb may be normal early in acute haemorrhage)
  - Group and screen (cross-match 4 units if unstable)
  - Rh status (critical for anti-D)
  - Serum β-hCG (quantitative — trend not needed for acute management of rupture, but baseline)
  - U+E, LFT, and clotting (if massive transfusion anticipated)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) — diagnostic if empty uterus + adnexal mass ± free fluid; can detect cardiac activity in ectopic (rare)
  - **Second-line:** transabdominal ultrasound (if TVS unavailable); diagnostic laparoscopy (gold standard if USS equivocal and patient stable)
  - **Never-miss test:** **Urine or serum β-hCG** in any woman aged 12–50 with abdominal pain or syncope. Omission = missed rupture with fatal consequences.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC:** secure airway if GCS <9, high-flow O₂ via non-rebreather, two large-bore IVs
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 500 mL–1L warmed crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) bolus; if no response after 2L → activate **massive transfusion protocol** (packed RBCs + FFP + platelets in 1:1:1 ratio) and call gynaecology reg + anaesthetist for urgent laparotomy
  - **Stop any oxytocin or uterotonics** (none given here unless mistaken)
  - **Analgesia:** IV morphine 2.5–5 mg titrated (if systolic BP >90 mmHg); avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk)
  - **Definitive:** Urgent **laparoscopic salpingectomy** (or salpingotomy if future fertility desired and stable) if haemodynamically stable; otherwise **open laparotomy** via Pfannenstiel or midline incision → salpingectomy and clot evacuation
  - **If massive haemorrhage:** aortic compression, cell salvage, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (check local policy)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Post-operative monitoring on ward or HDU depending on blood loss
  - Repeat FBC, U+E; monitor urine output
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** mechanical (anti-embolism stockings + intermittent pneumatic compression) until ambulatory; then enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if no contraindication
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** administer 250 IU IM within 72 hours if Rh-negative woman (NB: dose per NICE — 250 IU for all gestations; but [check local policy — some use 500 IU for >20 weeks])
  - Remove drain when output minimal
  - **Discharge planning:** advice about future fertility follow-up; avoid pregnancy for 3 months
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Confirm negative β-hCG (2–3 weeks post-op; check weekly until negative — usually <14 days)
  - Refer to fertility specialist if future pregnancy desired
  - Contraception advice (COCP can start after 4–6 weeks)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** maintain with Hartmann’s at 80 mL/h until tolerating oral fluids; if hypotensive, continue resuscitation
  - **NBM status:** NBM until surgical review (for potential laparotomy)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 chart every 15 minutes initially, then hourly for 4 hours, then 4-hourly if stable
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily from 12 hours post-op (if no haemorrhagic risk)
  - **Analgesia:** regular paracetamol 1g QDS + PRN IV morphine (for severe) or oral tramadol 50–100mg QDS (if tolerating oral)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** 250 IU IM (for Rh-negative women, within 72h of ectopic; irrespective of gestation) — one dose only
- **Morphine:** IV 2.5–5 mg stat, repeated every 5 minutes as needed (max 20mg in first hour) — use in shocked patients with caution
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 minutes (may reduce bleeding in massive haemorrhage — [check local massive transfusion protocol])
- **Oxytocin:** Not indicated in ectopic (but if need to control bleeding from an unexpected uterine site, give 5 IU slow IV bolus — but this is extremely rare)
- **Enoxaparin:** 40 mg SC daily for VTE prophylaxis (start 12h post-op if no bleeding concern)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs until haemostasis achieved
  - Morphine: avoid in shock if significant respiratory compromise
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid if active fibrinolytic state (e.g. DIC) without expert input
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (20 mg daily); morphine: avoid accumulation in renal failure (use fentanyl or lower doses)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Failing to give anti-D** to an Rh-negative woman — the single most harmful omission. Also: **using methotrexate in a ruptured ectopic** (can worsen haemorrhage) — only indicated in unruptured, stable ectopic.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock → multi-organ failure, ARDS
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Ureteral / bowel injury during surgery
  - Anaesthetic complications (aspiration, failed intubation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Tubal damage → reduced fertility (ipsilateral loss)
  - Recurrence risk: 10–15% (higher if salpingotomy vs salpingectomy)
  - Chronic pelvic pain (adhesions)
  - Psychological trauma / PTSD
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality <0.1% with prompt surgical care in the UK but remains leading cause of maternal death in first trimester. Fertility outcome: 60–80% pregnancy rate in the remaining tube within 2 years if no other pathology.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always** check Rh status and give anti-D — even if bleeding is minimal
- **Always** perform a urinary β-hCG in any woman with abdominal pain who is of reproductive age — the single most important diagnostic step
- **Use bedside ultrasound** (if trained) to detect free fluid and adnexal mass — it speeds diagnosis in the unstable patient
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT** rely on a negative transvaginal ultrasound to rule out ectopic — up to 5% of ectopics are not visualised; if pregnancy test positive and no IUP on USS, manage as ectopic until proven otherwise
- **Do NOT** delay surgery in an unstable patient for further imaging — go straight to laparotomy
- **Do NOT** give methotrexate to a patient with a ruptured ectopic — it is contraindicated in unstable patients and those with haemoperitoneum
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding (> pads per hour), fainting or dizziness, vomiting
- **Follow-up:** Gynaecology clinic in 2 weeks; ensure β-hCG checked weekly until negative (if any risk of retained trophoblast) — if not negative by day 14, re-refer
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (gynaecology reg + anaesthetist) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g. SBP <90 mmHg, HR >130, respiratory rate >25)
  - GCS <15 (especially if falling)
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - Unstable despite 1L crystalloid
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest / peri-arrest
  - Massive transfusion protocol activated
  - Persistent hypotension after 2L fluid + blood products
  - Need for inotropic support
- **Specific thresholds:** Lactate >4 mmol/L, base deficit <-6, SBP <80 mmHg despite fluids — activate massive transfusion and call most senior surgeon on call

rawMarkdown:
## Ectopic Pregnancy — Ruptured
**One-liner:** Haemorrhagic emergency from tubal rupture in a woman of reproductive age with positive pregnancy test — requires immediate surgical control of bleeding.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Haemorrhagic shock:** hypotension + tachycardia unresponsive to 2L crystalloid = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise. Single finding: postural hypotension (lying -> sitting BP drop >20 mmHg) with abdominal tenderness and positive pregnancy test.
- **Massive intra-abdominal bleeding:** loss of consciousness / peritonism / distended ballotable uterus sign (Cullen’s sign is late). Single finding: **free fluid in Morrison's pouch on FAST ultrasound** in a shocked pregnant woman.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Lower abdominal/pelvic pain (sudden, severe, unilateral → diffuse)
  - Amenorrhoea (6–8 weeks) with positive pregnancy test
  - Vaginal bleeding (often scant, brown, variable)
  - Syncope / presyncope / shoulder tip pain (due to phrenic irritation from blood)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal: tenderness ± rigidity, guarding, rebound; distension with ballotable uterus if large haemoperitoneum
  - Pelvic: cervical excitation (pulling of cervix causes sharp pain), adnexal tenderness ± mass
  - Vital signs: tachycardia ± hypotension (late sign), postural BP drop, narrow pulse pressure

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Anaesthetic ectopic / tubal abortion** → mild pain, no shock, small adnexal mass on USS
- **Miscarriage (threatened / incomplete)** → central pain, open cervical os, intrauterine products on USS
- **Ovarian cyst rupture** → sudden pain but negative pregnancy test (key differentiator)
- **Appendicitis** → migrating pain, anorexia, negative pregnancy test (or intrauterine pregnancy on USS)
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** → bilateral pain, cervical discharge, fever, positive STI screen
- **Ureteric colic** → flank-to-groin pain, haematuria, negative pregnancy test
  - **Splitter:** the most reliable single feature that bifurcates ruptured ectopic from all others is **positive pregnancy test + free fluid in pelvis** on bedside ultrasound. If positive test and no intrauterine pregnancy on transvaginal USS → ectopic until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine β-hCG (if serum not yet back) — **MUST do in every woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain**
  - Two large-bore IV cannulae
  - ECG (tachycardia, but not diagnostic)
  - Bedside FAST / limited pelvic ultrasound if trained (detect free fluid, adnexal mass)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia from occult bleed; Hb may be normal early in acute haemorrhage)
  - Group and screen (cross-match 4 units if unstable)
  - Rh status (critical for anti-D)
  - Serum β-hCG (quantitative — trend not needed for acute management of rupture, but baseline)
  - U+E, LFT, and clotting (if massive transfusion anticipated)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) — diagnostic if empty uterus + adnexal mass ± free fluid; can detect cardiac activity in ectopic (rare)
  - **Second-line:** transabdominal ultrasound (if TVS unavailable); diagnostic laparoscopy (gold standard if USS equivocal and patient stable)
  - **Never-miss test:** **Urine or serum β-hCG** in any woman aged 12–50 with abdominal pain or syncope. Omission = missed rupture with fatal consequences.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABC:** secure airway if GCS <9, high-flow O₂ via non-rebreather, two large-bore IVs
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** 500 mL–1L warmed crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) bolus; if no response after 2L → activate **massive transfusion protocol** (packed RBCs + FFP + platelets in 1:1:1 ratio) and call gynaecology reg + anaesthetist for urgent laparotomy
  - **Stop any oxytocin or uterotonics** (none given here unless mistaken)
  - **Analgesia:** IV morphine 2.5–5 mg titrated (if systolic BP >90 mmHg); avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk)
  - **Definitive:** Urgent **laparoscopic salpingectomy** (or salpingotomy if future fertility desired and stable) if haemodynamically stable; otherwise **open laparotomy** via Pfannenstiel or midline incision → salpingectomy and clot evacuation
  - **If massive haemorrhage:** aortic compression, cell salvage, consider tranexamic acid 1g IV (check local policy)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Post-operative monitoring on ward or HDU depending on blood loss
  - Repeat FBC, U+E; monitor urine output
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** mechanical (anti-embolism stockings + intermittent pneumatic compression) until ambulatory; then enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if no contraindication
  - **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** administer 250 IU IM within 72 hours if Rh-negative woman (NB: dose per NICE — 250 IU for all gestations; but [check local policy — some use 500 IU for >20 weeks])
  - Remove drain when output minimal
  - **Discharge planning:** advice about future fertility follow-up; avoid pregnancy for 3 months
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Confirm negative β-hCG (2–3 weeks post-op; check weekly until negative — usually <14 days)
  - Refer to fertility specialist if future pregnancy desired
  - Contraception advice (COCP can start after 4–6 weeks)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** maintain with Hartmann’s at 80 mL/h until tolerating oral fluids; if hypotensive, continue resuscitation
  - **NBM status:** NBM until surgical review (for potential laparotomy)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 chart every 15 minutes initially, then hourly for 4 hours, then 4-hourly if stable
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily from 12 hours post-op (if no haemorrhagic risk)
  - **Analgesia:** regular paracetamol 1g QDS + PRN IV morphine (for severe) or oral tramadol 50–100mg QDS (if tolerating oral)

### Drugs & Doses
- **Anti-D immunoglobulin:** 250 IU IM (for Rh-negative women, within 72h of ectopic; irrespective of gestation) — one dose only
- **Morphine:** IV 2.5–5 mg stat, repeated every 5 minutes as needed (max 20mg in first hour) — use in shocked patients with caution
- **Tranexamic acid:** 1g IV over 10 minutes (may reduce bleeding in massive haemorrhage — [check local massive transfusion protocol])
- **Oxytocin:** Not indicated in ectopic (but if need to control bleeding from an unexpected uterine site, give 5 IU slow IV bolus — but this is extremely rare)
- **Enoxaparin:** 40 mg SC daily for VTE prophylaxis (start 12h post-op if no bleeding concern)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Avoid NSAIDs until haemostasis achieved
  - Morphine: avoid in shock if significant respiratory compromise
  - Tranexamic acid: avoid if active fibrinolytic state (e.g. DIC) without expert input
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (20 mg daily); morphine: avoid accumulation in renal failure (use fentanyl or lower doses)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Failing to give anti-D** to an Rh-negative woman — the single most harmful omission. Also: **using methotrexate in a ruptured ectopic** (can worsen haemorrhage) — only indicated in unruptured, stable ectopic.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Haemorrhagic shock → multi-organ failure, ARDS
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Ureteral / bowel injury during surgery
  - Anaesthetic complications (aspiration, failed intubation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Tubal damage → reduced fertility (ipsilateral loss)
  - Recurrence risk: 10–15% (higher if salpingotomy vs salpingectomy)
  - Chronic pelvic pain (adhesions)
  - Psychological trauma / PTSD

### Prognosis
- Mortality <0.1% with prompt surgical care in the UK but remains leading cause of maternal death in first trimester. Fertility outcome: 60–80% pregnancy rate in the remaining tube within 2 years if no other pathology.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always** check Rh status and give anti-D — even if bleeding is minimal
- **Always** perform a urinary β-hCG in any woman with abdominal pain who is of reproductive age — the single most important diagnostic step
- **Use bedside ultrasound** (if trained) to detect free fluid and adnexal mass — it speeds diagnosis in the unstable patient

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT** rely on a negative transvaginal ultrasound to rule out ectopic — up to 5% of ectopics are not visualised; if pregnancy test positive and no IUP on USS, manage as ectopic until proven otherwise
- **Do NOT** delay surgery in an unstable patient for further imaging — go straight to laparotomy
- **Do NOT** give methotrexate to a patient with a ruptured ectopic — it is contraindicated in unstable patients and those with haemoperitoneum

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening abdominal pain, heavy vaginal bleeding (> pads per hour), fainting or dizziness, vomiting
- **Follow-up:** Gynaecology clinic in 2 weeks; ensure β-hCG checked weekly until negative (if any risk of retained trophoblast) — if not negative by day 14, re-refer

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (gynaecology reg + anaesthetist) immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter red (e.g. SBP <90 mmHg, HR >130, respiratory rate >25)
  - GCS <15 (especially if falling)
  - Lactate >2.5 mmol/L
  - Unstable despite 1L crystalloid
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest / peri-arrest
  - Massive transfusion protocol activated
  - Persistent hypotension after 2L fluid + blood products
  - Need for inotropic support
- **Specific thresholds:** Lactate >4 mmol/L, base deficit <-6, SBP <80 mmHg despite fluids — activate massive transfusion and call most senior surgeon on call
